title: Torts
subject: Torts
summary: Torts asks whether someone wrongfully harmed another person, property, reputation, privacy, business, or legal rights.

sections.title: Big Picture
body: Did someone wrongfully harm another person, property, reputation, privacy, business, or legal rights?
The first thing to identify is the type of tort, because each tort has different rules.
Wrongful use of the legal system

blocks.text: Did someone wrongfully harm another person, property, reputation, privacy, business, or legal rights?
The first thing to identify is the type of tort, because each tort has different rules.
Wrongful use of the legal system
Exam tip: Analyze each element separately and do not skip damages.
Rule: Defendant must act as a reasonably prudent person under similar circumstances.
- Physical disability: reasonable person with same disability.
- Child: child of similar age, intelligence, and experience.
- Child performing dangerous adult activity: adult standard.
- Professional: reasonably competent member of the profession.
Rule: There is generally no duty to rescue.
Key distinction: A person who begins a rescue must act reasonably and not leave the victim worse off.
Rule: Use but-for causation unless multiple sufficient causes independently would have produced the harm.
Multiple sufficient causes: Use substantial-factor or equivalent principles recognized by the question.
Exam tip: Ask whether the injury would have occurred when and as it did without defendant’s conduct.
Rule: Defendant is liable for foreseeable types of harm resulting from the risk created.
Superseding cause: Extraordinary and unforeseeable event that breaks the causal chain.
Exam tip: Focus on foreseeability of the type of harm, not the exact sequence.
Pure comparative negligence: Recovery reduced by plaintiff’s percentage of fault.
Modified comparative negligence: Plaintiff barred at 50% or more than 50%, depending on jurisdiction.
Contributory negligence: Any plaintiff negligence is a complete bar in minority jurisdictions, subject to exceptions.
Assumption of risk: Plaintiff knowingly and voluntarily encounters a known risk.
Rule: Employer is vicariously liable for an employee’s tort committed within the scope of employment.
- Kind of conduct employee was hired to perform.
- At least partly motivated to serve employer.
Detour: Minor deviation; liability may continue.
Frolic: Major personal departure; employer generally not liable.
Intentional torts: May fall within scope when foreseeable from employment, authorized, or motivated to serve employer.
Independent contractors: No general vicarious liability, subject to nondelegable duties and inherently dangerous activities.
Wild animals: Strict liability for harm caused by dangerous characteristics.
Domestic animals: Strict liability when owner knew or should have known of dangerous propensity.
Key distinction: Liability extends only to the kind of harm making the activity abnormally dangerous.
3. Defect existed when product left defendant.
4. Product reached user without substantial change.
- Manufacturing defect: product departs from intended design.
- Design defect: design is unreasonably dangerous under applicable test.
- Warning defect: inadequate warning of nonobvious risk.
Key distinction: Contractual privity is not required.
1. Statute sets a standard of conduct.
3. Injury is the type statute sought to prevent.
Exam tip: Negligence per se usually establishes duty and breach, not causation or damages.
Zone-of-danger theory: Plaintiff was personally threatened with physical harm and suffered serious emotional distress.
Exam tip: Rules vary, so use the standard supplied or implied by the question.
Rule: Defendant takes the plaintiff as found and is liable for the full extent of harm once the general type of injury is foreseeable.
Key distinction: Defendant need not foresee the plaintiff’s unusual vulnerability.

sections.title: 1. Intentional Torts
body: A person acts intentionally if they either:
Want the result to happen, or
Know the result is substantially certain to happen
Maya pulls a chair away right as Leo is sitting down.
“I only meant it as a joke.”
But she still intended the act that caused the contact.

blocks.text: A person acts intentionally if they either:
Want the result to happen, or
Know the result is substantially certain to happen
Maya pulls a chair away right as Leo is sitting down.
“I only meant it as a joke.”
But she still intended the act that caused the contact.

sections.title: 2. Transferred Intent
body: Transferred intent applies when a defendant intends one intentional tort against one person but accidentally harms someone else or causes another covered tort.
Maya throws a marker intending to hit Leo.
Maya’s intent transfers from Leo to Noah.
Bad intent can follow the harm.

blocks.text: Transferred intent applies when a defendant intends one intentional tort against one person but accidentally harms someone else or causes another covered tort.
Maya throws a marker intending to hit Leo.
Maya’s intent transfers from Leo to Noah.
Bad intent can follow the harm.

sections.title: 3. Battery
body: Intentional harmful or offensive contact with another person.
Battery requires intent to cause contact.
It does not require intent to injure.
Maya intentionally shoves Leo lightly as a joke.
Leo falls and breaks his wrist.
This is battery because Maya intended the contact.
Maya accidentally bumps Leo while turning around.
That may be negligence, but not battery.
Contact with something connected to the person can count.

blocks.text: Intentional harmful or offensive contact with another person.
Battery requires intent to cause contact.
It does not require intent to injure.
Maya intentionally shoves Leo lightly as a joke.
Leo falls and breaks his wrist.
This is battery because Maya intended the contact.
Maya accidentally bumps Leo while turning around.
That may be negligence, but not battery.
Contact with something connected to the person can count.

sections.title: 4. Assault
body: Intentionally causing someone to reasonably anticipate imminent harmful or offensive contact.
Plaintiff must be aware of the threat.
The threatened contact must be imminent.
Maya raises a metal bat like she is about to swing at Leo.
Leo sees it and expects to be hit.
Usually not assault because the threat is not imminent.
Assault = “I think I’m about to be touched.”

blocks.text: Intentionally causing someone to reasonably anticipate imminent harmful or offensive contact.
Plaintiff must be aware of the threat.
The threatened contact must be imminent.
Maya raises a metal bat like she is about to swing at Leo.
Leo sees it and expects to be hit.
Usually not assault because the threat is not imminent.
Assault = “I think I’m about to be touched.”

sections.title: 5. Battery vs. Assault
body: Maya swings at Leo and misses.
Maya hits Leo while he is sleeping.
Maya swings, Leo sees it coming, and Maya hits him.
Battery = contact. Assault = seeing contact coming.

blocks.text: Maya swings at Leo and misses.
Maya hits Leo while he is sleeping.
Maya swings, Leo sees it coming, and Maya hits him.
Battery = contact. Assault = seeing contact coming.

sections.title: 6. Intentional Infliction of Emotional Distress
body: Conduct that goes beyond normal insults, rudeness, or threats.
It must be intolerable in a civilized society.
Maya falsely tells Leo that his child was seriously injured just to emotionally destroy him.
Conduct is more likely outrageous when:

blocks.text: Conduct that goes beyond normal insults, rudeness, or threats.
It must be intolerable in a civilized society.
Maya falsely tells Leo that his child was seriously injured just to emotionally destroy him.
Conduct is more likely outrageous when:

sections.title: 7. False Imprisonment
body: Intentional confinement within a limited area, where the plaintiff is aware of the confinement.
Plaintiff is conscious of the confinement
Maya locks Leo inside a storage room and blocks the only safe exit.
If Leo knows there is an unlocked safe exit, he is not confined.
Leo does not have to escape through a dangerous exit.
Leo does not need to jump from a second-floor window.
False imprisonment = trapped with no reasonable safe way out.

blocks.text: Intentional confinement within a limited area, where the plaintiff is aware of the confinement.
Plaintiff is conscious of the confinement
Maya locks Leo inside a storage room and blocks the only safe exit.
If Leo knows there is an unlocked safe exit, he is not confined.
Leo does not have to escape through a dangerous exit.
Leo does not need to jump from a second-floor window.
False imprisonment = trapped with no reasonable safe way out.

sections.title: 8. Consent
body: Consent is a defense to intentional torts.
If plaintiff legally consented, defendant is usually not liable.
Leo agrees to spar with Maya in boxing class.
Maya punches him within the rules.
Consent only works within its limits.
Maya suddenly hits him with a chair.
Consent works only within its scope.

blocks.text: Consent is a defense to intentional torts.
If plaintiff legally consented, defendant is usually not liable.
Leo agrees to spar with Maya in boxing class.
Maya punches him within the rules.
Consent only works within its limits.
Maya suddenly hits him with a chair.
Consent works only within its scope.

sections.title: 9. Self-Defense
body: A person may use reasonable force to protect against an immediate unlawful threat.
Leo raises his fist and steps toward Maya.
If Leo hits Maya and walks away, Maya cannot chase him later and hit him back.
Deadly force requires reasonable belief of:
Self-defense stops danger. It does not punish the past.

blocks.text: A person may use reasonable force to protect against an immediate unlawful threat.
Leo raises his fist and steps toward Maya.
If Leo hits Maya and walks away, Maya cannot chase him later and hit him back.
Deadly force requires reasonable belief of:
Self-defense stops danger. It does not punish the past.

sections.title: 10. Defense of Others
body: A person may use reasonable force to defend another person if they reasonably believe that person has the right to self-defense.
Maya sees Leo about to punch Noah.
That may be defense of others.
Defense of others = reasonable rescue from immediate force.

blocks.text: A person may use reasonable force to defend another person if they reasonably believe that person has the right to self-defense.
Maya sees Leo about to punch Noah.
That may be defense of others.
Defense of others = reasonable rescue from immediate force.

sections.title: 11. Defense of Property
body: A person may use reasonable, nondeadly force to protect property.
Maya sees Leo grabbing her laptop.
She pulls it back and blocks him.
Deadly force cannot be used merely to protect property.
If someone wrongfully occupies land, the owner usually must use legal process.
Property is not worth deadly force.

blocks.text: A person may use reasonable, nondeadly force to protect property.
Maya sees Leo grabbing her laptop.
She pulls it back and blocks him.
Deadly force cannot be used merely to protect property.
If someone wrongfully occupies land, the owner usually must use legal process.
Property is not worth deadly force.

sections.title: 12. Merchant’s Privilege
body: A merchant may briefly detain someone suspected of theft if the merchant acts reasonably.
Reasonable belief of theft or nonpayment
Detention on or near the premises
A bookstore employee sees Leo hide a book in his coat.
The employee stops him near the exit and asks him to wait briefly.
Merchant can reasonably stop, not abuse.

blocks.text: A merchant may briefly detain someone suspected of theft if the merchant acts reasonably.
Reasonable belief of theft or nonpayment
Detention on or near the premises
A bookstore employee sees Leo hide a book in his coat.
The employee stops him near the exit and asks him to wait briefly.
Merchant can reasonably stop, not abuse.

sections.title: 13. Trespass to Chattels
body: Intentional interference with someone’s personal property.
Maya takes Leo’s tablet and uses it for ten minutes without permission.
That can be trespass to chattels.
Trespass to chattels = messing with someone’s stuff.

blocks.text: Intentional interference with someone’s personal property.
Maya takes Leo’s tablet and uses it for ten minutes without permission.
That can be trespass to chattels.
Trespass to chattels = messing with someone’s stuff.

sections.title: 14. Conversion
body: Serious interference with someone’s personal property, so serious that defendant must pay full value.
Maya takes Leo’s laptop, sells it, and keeps the money.
Plaintiff can recover the full value of the property at the time of conversion.

blocks.text: Serious interference with someone’s personal property, so serious that defendant must pay full value.
Maya takes Leo’s laptop, sells it, and keeps the money.
Plaintiff can recover the full value of the property at the time of conversion.

sections.title: 15. Trespass to Land
body: Intentional physical invasion of another’s land.
Defendant does not need to know the land belongs to someone else.
Defendant only needs to intend to enter that physical place.
Maya walks onto a garden because she thinks it is public land.
It is actually Leo’s private land.
If Maya accidentally sends something onto Leo’s land without intending that physical invasion, it may be negligence instead of trespass.
Mistaken entry = trespass. Accidental entry = maybe negligence.

blocks.text: Intentional physical invasion of another’s land.
Defendant does not need to know the land belongs to someone else.
Defendant only needs to intend to enter that physical place.
Maya walks onto a garden because she thinks it is public land.
It is actually Leo’s private land.
If Maya accidentally sends something onto Leo’s land without intending that physical invasion, it may be negligence instead of trespass.
Mistaken entry = trespass. Accidental entry = maybe negligence.

sections.title: 16. Necessity
body: Necessity is a defense to property torts when defendant enters or uses property to avoid serious harm.
Private necessity protects a small group or private interest.
Defendant may enter, but must pay actual damage.
Maya is caught in a dangerous snowstorm and enters Leo’s empty cabin to avoid freezing.
She may be privileged to enter.
But if she breaks the door, she must pay for the door.
Private necessity = allowed in, but pay actual damage.
Public necessity protects the public from a larger emergency.
If reasonable, defendant usually is not liable for damage.
A firefighter destroys a storage shed to stop a fire from reaching several homes.
Public necessity = public emergency, no pay.

blocks.text: Necessity is a defense to property torts when defendant enters or uses property to avoid serious harm.
Private necessity protects a small group or private interest.
Defendant may enter, but must pay actual damage.
Maya is caught in a dangerous snowstorm and enters Leo’s empty cabin to avoid freezing.
She may be privileged to enter.
But if she breaks the door, she must pay for the door.
Private necessity = allowed in, but pay actual damage.
Public necessity protects the public from a larger emergency.
If reasonable, defendant usually is not liable for damage.
A firefighter destroys a storage shed to stop a fire from reaching several homes.
Public necessity = public emergency, no pay.

sections.title: 17. Private Nuisance
body: Substantial and unreasonable interference with another person’s use and enjoyment of land.
Maya runs loud machinery every night behind Leo’s house.
The noise shakes the walls and keeps Leo awake.
Interference with use and enjoyment of land
It must bother a normal reasonable person.
Private nuisance = messes with my land enjoyment.

blocks.text: Substantial and unreasonable interference with another person’s use and enjoyment of land.
Maya runs loud machinery every night behind Leo’s house.
The noise shakes the walls and keeps Leo awake.
Interference with use and enjoyment of land
It must bother a normal reasonable person.
Private nuisance = messes with my land enjoyment.

sections.title: 18. Public Nuisance
body: Unreasonable interference with a right common to the general public.
A private person can sue only if they suffer harm different in kind from the general public.
Everyone is delayed by a blocked public road.
But if a falling barrier damages Leo’s car, Leo suffered special harm.
Public nuisance = community harm. Private plaintiff needs special harm.

blocks.text: Unreasonable interference with a right common to the general public.
A private person can sue only if they suffer harm different in kind from the general public.
Everyone is delayed by a blocked public road.
But if a falling barrier damages Leo’s car, Leo suffered special harm.
Public nuisance = community harm. Private plaintiff needs special harm.

sections.title: 19. Negligence
body: Negligence is careless conduct causing actual harm.
Did defendant have to be careful?
Did the carelessness cause the harm?
Maya leaves a sharp tool on a gym floor.
Leo steps on it and is injured.

blocks.text: Negligence is careless conduct causing actual harm.
Did defendant have to be careful?
Did the carelessness cause the harm?
Maya leaves a sharp tool on a gym floor.
Leo steps on it and is injured.

sections.title: 20. Duty
body: People generally owe reasonable care to foreseeable plaintiffs.
Maya throws heavy boxes off a balcony.
People walking below are foreseeable plaintiffs.
Usually, a person has no legal duty to help.
Maya sees Leo faint on the sidewalk.
She usually has no duty to help.
A duty to act may arise if:
No duty to start helping, but once you start or create danger, act reasonably.

blocks.text: People generally owe reasonable care to foreseeable plaintiffs.
Maya throws heavy boxes off a balcony.
People walking below are foreseeable plaintiffs.
Usually, a person has no legal duty to help.
Maya sees Leo faint on the sidewalk.
She usually has no duty to help.
A duty to act may arise if:
No duty to start helping, but once you start or create danger, act reasonably.

sections.title: 21. Standard of Care
body: Did this defendant try their best?
What would a reasonable person have done?
A blind person is judged as a reasonable blind person.
Mental or emotional disability usually does not lower the standard.
Children are judged as reasonable children of similar age, intelligence, and experience.
Children doing adult dangerous activities are judged like adults.
Reasonable person, not this person’s personal best.

blocks.text: Did this defendant try their best?
What would a reasonable person have done?
A blind person is judged as a reasonable blind person.
Mental or emotional disability usually does not lower the standard.
Children are judged as reasonable children of similar age, intelligence, and experience.
Children doing adult dangerous activities are judged like adults.
Reasonable person, not this person’s personal best.

sections.title: 22. Professional Negligence
body: Professionals must act like ordinary professionals in the same field.
Professional negligence usually needs expert testimony to explain the standard of care.
No expert is needed if the mistake is obvious.
An architect uses cardboard columns to support a building.
A jury does not need an expert to know that is careless.
Professional negligence usually needs expert testimony unless the mistake is obvious.

blocks.text: Professionals must act like ordinary professionals in the same field.
Professional negligence usually needs expert testimony to explain the standard of care.
No expert is needed if the mistake is obvious.
An architect uses cardboard columns to support a building.
A jury does not need an expert to know that is careless.
Professional negligence usually needs expert testimony unless the mistake is obvious.

sections.title: 23. Land Possessor Duties
body: Usually, land possessor must not intentionally, willfully, wantonly, or recklessly hurt them.
Trespasser gets little protection, but no traps.
If the landowner knows trespassers regularly enter, the landowner must warn about hidden dangerous artificial conditions.
Children regularly cut across Maya’s yard.
Maya has a hidden electrified wire.
She may need to warn or protect.
A land possessor may be liable for injuries to child trespassers if:
Burden of fixing is small compared to danger
Landowner fails to use reasonable care
Kids + artificial danger + foreseeable trespass = attractive nuisance.
A licensee enters with permission, but not for business.
Maya knows one stair is loose.
Licensee = warn about hidden dangers you know.
An invitee enters for business or public purpose.
Reasonable care, including duty to inspect and make safe.
A café should reasonably inspect for spills where customers walk.

blocks.text: Usually, land possessor must not intentionally, willfully, wantonly, or recklessly hurt them.
Trespasser gets little protection, but no traps.
If the landowner knows trespassers regularly enter, the landowner must warn about hidden dangerous artificial conditions.
Children regularly cut across Maya’s yard.
Maya has a hidden electrified wire.
She may need to warn or protect.
A land possessor may be liable for injuries to child trespassers if:
Burden of fixing is small compared to danger
Landowner fails to use reasonable care
Kids + artificial danger + foreseeable trespass = attractive nuisance.
A licensee enters with permission, but not for business.
Maya knows one stair is loose.
Licensee = warn about hidden dangers you know.
An invitee enters for business or public purpose.
Reasonable care, including duty to inspect and make safe.
A café should reasonably inspect for spills where customers walk.

sections.title: 24. Landlord Liability
body: Once tenant takes possession, tenant usually becomes responsible for conditions on the property.
Hidden dangers landlord failed to disclose
A broken lobby tile injures a visitor.
Landlord liable where landlord still controls, promised, hid, or caused the problem.

blocks.text: Once tenant takes possession, tenant usually becomes responsible for conditions on the property.
Hidden dangers landlord failed to disclose
A broken lobby tile injures a visitor.
Landlord liable where landlord still controls, promised, hid, or caused the problem.

sections.title: 25. Breach
body: Breach occurs when defendant fails to meet the required standard of care.
Compare defendant to a reasonable person under the circumstances.
How hard would it be to prevent?
Maya leaves boiling liquid on the edge of a table at a children’s party.
High risk + serious harm + easy fix = breach.

blocks.text: Breach occurs when defendant fails to meet the required standard of care.
Compare defendant to a reasonable person under the circumstances.
How hard would it be to prevent?
Maya leaves boiling liquid on the edge of a table at a children’s party.
High risk + serious harm + easy fix = breach.

sections.title: 26. Custom
body: Custom is admissible evidence of reasonable care, but it is not conclusive.
Every warehouse stacks boxes dangerously high.
A box falls and injures Leo.
The warehouse cannot automatically win by saying:
The entire industry may be negligent.
Custom helps, but custom does not control.

blocks.text: Custom is admissible evidence of reasonable care, but it is not conclusive.
Every warehouse stacks boxes dangerously high.
A box falls and injures Leo.
The warehouse cannot automatically win by saying:
The entire industry may be negligent.
Custom helps, but custom does not control.

sections.title: 27. Negligence Per Se
body: Negligence per se means a statute supplies the standard of care.
Plaintiff is in the protected class
Harm is the type the statute was designed to prevent
A rule says restaurants must cover open floor drains to prevent customer falls.
If the statute is about tax paperwork, and plaintiff slips on a floor, the statute does not match the harm.
Statute violation = duty/breach shortcut, if plaintiff and harm match.

blocks.text: Negligence per se means a statute supplies the standard of care.
Plaintiff is in the protected class
Harm is the type the statute was designed to prevent
A rule says restaurants must cover open floor drains to prevent customer falls.
If the statute is about tax paperwork, and plaintiff slips on a floor, the statute does not match the harm.
Statute violation = duty/breach shortcut, if plaintiff and harm match.

sections.title: 28. Res Ipsa Loquitur
body: The jury may infer negligence when the accident usually would not happen without negligence.
Accident usually does not happen without negligence
Leo walks past a bakery and a rolling pin falls from an upstairs window onto him.
Rolling pins usually do not fall from windows unless someone was careless.
Res ipsa usually gets the case to the jury.
It does not automatically mean plaintiff wins.
Res ipsa = this probably happened because someone was careless.

blocks.text: The jury may infer negligence when the accident usually would not happen without negligence.
Accident usually does not happen without negligence
Leo walks past a bakery and a rolling pin falls from an upstairs window onto him.
Rolling pins usually do not fall from windows unless someone was careless.
Res ipsa usually gets the case to the jury.
It does not automatically mean plaintiff wins.
Res ipsa = this probably happened because someone was careless.

sections.title: 29. Causation
body: But for defendant’s conduct, would the harm have happened?
Maya leaves a cord across a hallway.
But for the cord, Leo would not have fallen.
Was the harm foreseeable and within the risk created by defendant?
Leaving a cord across a hallway creates a risk that someone will trip.
Actual cause = did it cause it? Proximate cause = is it fair to blame defendant?

blocks.text: But for defendant’s conduct, would the harm have happened?
Maya leaves a cord across a hallway.
But for the cord, Leo would not have fallen.
Was the harm foreseeable and within the risk created by defendant?
Leaving a cord across a hallway creates a risk that someone will trip.
Actual cause = did it cause it? Proximate cause = is it fair to blame defendant?

sections.title: 30. Intervening and Superseding Causes
body: An event that happens after defendant’s negligence but before the final injury.
An unforeseeable intervening cause that cuts off liability.
If defendant’s duty was to protect against that kind of criminal act, the criminal act does not cut off liability.
Foreseeable intervening = still liable. Unforeseeable superseding = cuts off.

blocks.text: An event that happens after defendant’s negligence but before the final injury.
An unforeseeable intervening cause that cuts off liability.
If defendant’s duty was to protect against that kind of criminal act, the criminal act does not cut off liability.
Foreseeable intervening = still liable. Unforeseeable superseding = cuts off.

sections.title: 31. Eggshell Plaintiff Rule
body: Defendant takes plaintiff as they find them.
The type of harm must be foreseeable.
The extent of harm does not need to be foreseeable.
Leo has a rare bone condition and suffers a severe injury.
Maya is liable for the full injury.
You break it, you bought all the damage.

blocks.text: Defendant takes plaintiff as they find them.
The type of harm must be foreseeable.
The extent of harm does not need to be foreseeable.
Leo has a rare bone condition and suffers a severe injury.
Maya is liable for the full injury.
You break it, you bought all the damage.

sections.title: 32. Damages in Negligence
body: Usually not recoverable in ordinary negligence without physical injury or property damage.

blocks.text: Usually not recoverable in ordinary negligence without physical injury or property damage.

sections.title: 33. Negligent Infliction of Emotional Distress
body: A sign falls and narrowly misses Leo.
Plaintiff sees a close family member injured.
Some relationships allow recovery without physical danger.
A funeral home loses a family member’s ashes.
NIED = danger zone, close-family bystander, or special relationship.

blocks.text: A sign falls and narrowly misses Leo.
Plaintiff sees a close family member injured.
Some relationships allow recovery without physical danger.
A funeral home loses a family member’s ashes.
NIED = danger zone, close-family bystander, or special relationship.

sections.title: 34. Vicarious Liability
body: Vicarious liability means one person is liable for another person’s tort because of their relationship.
Employer is liable for employee’s torts within scope of employment.
A bakery delivery employee negligently injures a pedestrian while delivering pastries.
Employee on the job = employer may pay.

blocks.text: Vicarious liability means one person is liable for another person’s tort because of their relationship.
Employer is liable for employee’s torts within scope of employment.
A bakery delivery employee negligently injures a pedestrian while delivering pastries.
Employee on the job = employer may pay.

sections.title: 35. Independent Contractors
body: A person who hires an independent contractor is usually not vicariously liable.
A grocery store hires a contractor to polish floors.
Contractor leaves the entrance dangerously slick.
Store may still be liable because keeping premises safe for customers is nondelegable.
You can delegate work, but not certain duties.

blocks.text: A person who hires an independent contractor is usually not vicariously liable.
A grocery store hires a contractor to polish floors.
Contractor leaves the entrance dangerously slick.
Store may still be liable because keeping premises safe for customers is nondelegable.
You can delegate work, but not certain duties.

sections.title: 36. Parents and Children
body: Parents are not automatically liable for their child’s torts.
Parents can be liable if they fail to reasonably control a child when they know control is needed.
Parent knows child keeps throwing bricks from a balcony and does nothing.
Parent not automatically liable, but liable for bad supervision.

blocks.text: Parents are not automatically liable for their child’s torts.
Parents can be liable if they fail to reasonably control a child when they know control is needed.
Parent knows child keeps throwing bricks from a balcony and does nothing.
Parent not automatically liable, but liable for bad supervision.

sections.title: 37. Joint and Several Liability
body: If multiple defendants cause one indivisible injury, plaintiff may collect the whole judgment from one defendant.
Two careless contractors cause one ceiling collapse.
Plaintiff may collect all damages from either defendant unless jurisdiction limits joint and several liability.
Joint and several = plaintiff can collect all from one.

blocks.text: If multiple defendants cause one indivisible injury, plaintiff may collect the whole judgment from one defendant.
Two careless contractors cause one ceiling collapse.
Plaintiff may collect all damages from either defendant unless jurisdiction limits joint and several liability.
Joint and several = plaintiff can collect all from one.

sections.title: 38. Contribution and Indemnity
body: One defendant pays more than their fair share and seeks partial reimbursement.
One defendant shifts the entire loss to another.
Employer may seek indemnity from employee.
Contribution = split the bill. Indemnity = shift the whole bill.

blocks.text: One defendant pays more than their fair share and seeks partial reimbursement.
One defendant shifts the entire loss to another.
Employer may seek indemnity from employee.
Contribution = split the bill. Indemnity = shift the whole bill.

sections.title: 39. Comparative and Contributory Negligence
body: Plaintiff is even slightly at fault.
Plaintiff always recovers defendant’s percentage of fault.
Plaintiff recovers only if not too at fault.
Contributory = one mistake can bar. Pure comparative = always reduced. Modified = reduced unless plaintiff too responsible.

blocks.text: Plaintiff is even slightly at fault.
Plaintiff always recovers defendant’s percentage of fault.
Plaintiff recovers only if not too at fault.
Contributory = one mistake can bar. Pure comparative = always reduced. Modified = reduced unless plaintiff too responsible.

sections.title: 40. Assumption of Risk
body: Plaintiff knowingly and voluntarily accepts a specific risk.
Plaintiff knowingly proceeds despite a specific risk.
Leo sees a gym machine is broken and still uses it for fun.
His recovery may be barred or reduced.
Known risk + voluntary choice = assumption of risk.

blocks.text: Plaintiff knowingly and voluntarily accepts a specific risk.
Plaintiff knowingly proceeds despite a specific risk.
Leo sees a gym machine is broken and still uses it for fun.
His recovery may be barred or reduced.
Known risk + voluntary choice = assumption of risk.

sections.title: 41. Strict Liability
body: Strict liability means defendant can be liable even if careful.

blocks.text: Strict liability means defendant can be liable even if careful.

sections.title: 42. Abnormally Dangerous Activities
body: Strict liability applies when an activity:
Creates a highly significant risk even with reasonable care
Strict liability applies only if the harm comes from the danger that makes the activity dangerous.
A company stores toxic gas and gas escapes.
But if an employee carrying a gas container drops a clipboard on someone’s foot, that is not the special toxic-gas risk.
Dangerous even when careful = strict liability.

blocks.text: Strict liability applies when an activity:
Creates a highly significant risk even with reasonable care
Strict liability applies only if the harm comes from the danger that makes the activity dangerous.
A company stores toxic gas and gas escapes.
But if an employee carrying a gas container drops a clipboard on someone’s foot, that is not the special toxic-gas risk.
Dangerous even when careful = strict liability.

sections.title: 43. Animals
body: Owner is strictly liable for harm from characteristic dangerous behavior.
Wild stays wild, even if trained.
Usually negligence unless owner knows the animal has abnormal dangerous tendencies.
Maya’s dog has repeatedly attacked visitors.
If the dog bites someone, strict liability may apply.
Owner or possessor of livestock-like animals is strictly liable for foreseeable property damage caused while trespassing.
Animal liability follows the animal owner or possessor.

blocks.text: Owner is strictly liable for harm from characteristic dangerous behavior.
Wild stays wild, even if trained.
Usually negligence unless owner knows the animal has abnormal dangerous tendencies.
Maya’s dog has repeatedly attacked visitors.
If the dog bites someone, strict liability may apply.
Owner or possessor of livestock-like animals is strictly liable for foreseeable property damage caused while trespassing.
Animal liability follows the animal owner or possessor.

sections.title: 44. Products Liability
body: Manufacturing = bad one. Design = bad plan. Warning = bad instructions.

blocks.text: Manufacturing = bad one. Design = bad plan. Warning = bad instructions.

sections.title: 45. Manufacturing Defect
body: A manufacturing defect means this one product came out differently from intended.
One heater has a loose internal wire because of an assembly mistake.
Manufacturing defect = this one is different from the rest.

blocks.text: A manufacturing defect means this one product came out differently from intended.
One heater has a loose internal wire because of an assembly mistake.
Manufacturing defect = this one is different from the rest.

sections.title: 46. Design Defect
body: A design defect means the whole product line was designed dangerously.
Is the product more dangerous than ordinary consumers would expect?
Do the risks outweigh the benefits?
Often requires a reasonable alternative design.
A foldable ladder is designed without locking hinges.
Every ladder of that model can collapse.

blocks.text: A design defect means the whole product line was designed dangerously.
Is the product more dangerous than ordinary consumers would expect?
Do the risks outweigh the benefits?
Often requires a reasonable alternative design.
A foldable ladder is designed without locking hinges.
Every ladder of that model can collapse.

sections.title: 47. Failure to Warn
body: A product has a warning defect if:
A reasonable warning could reduce the risk
A cleaning spray creates dangerous fumes near heat, but the label says nothing.

blocks.text: A product has a warning defect if:
A reasonable warning could reduce the risk
A cleaning spray creates dangerous fumes near heat, but the label says nothing.

sections.title: 48. Products Liability Defendants
body: Maya sells her old blender at a garage sale.
She is not a strict products liability defendant.
Business seller yes. Casual seller no.

blocks.text: Maya sells her old blender at a garage sale.
She is not a strict products liability defendant.
Business seller yes. Casual seller no.

sections.title: 49. Products Damages
body: Damage only to the product itself
A defective toaster burns Leo’s kitchen wall.
If the toaster simply stops working, that is warranty or contract.
People/property damage = tort. Product disappointment = warranty.

blocks.text: Damage only to the product itself
A defective toaster burns Leo’s kitchen wall.
If the toaster simply stops working, that is warranty or contract.
People/property damage = tort. Product disappointment = warranty.

sections.title: 50. Warranties
body: Product is fit for ordinary use.
A chair sold by a furniture store should support normal sitting.
Seller knows buyer’s special purpose and buyer relies on seller.
I need gloves safe for handling very cold materials.
The clerk recommends gloves that fail.
This refrigerator keeps food cold for 24 hours during a power outage.
Merchantability = normal use. Fitness = special use. Express = factual promise.

blocks.text: Product is fit for ordinary use.
A chair sold by a furniture store should support normal sitting.
Seller knows buyer’s special purpose and buyer relies on seller.
I need gloves safe for handling very cold materials.
The clerk recommends gloves that fail.
This refrigerator keeps food cold for 24 hours during a power outage.
Merchantability = normal use. Fitness = special use. Express = factual promise.

sections.title: 51. Defamation
body: Defamation = false reputation harm told to someone else.

blocks.text: Defamation = false reputation harm told to someone else.

sections.title: 52. Publication
body: Publication means communication to a third person who understands it.
It does not require a newspaper or website.
Maya whispers a false statement about Leo to Noah.
Publication = told to someone besides plaintiff.

blocks.text: Publication means communication to a third person who understands it.
It does not require a newspaper or website.
Maya whispers a false statement about Leo to Noah.
Publication = told to someone besides plaintiff.

sections.title: 53. Public Figures and Actual Malice
body: Public officials and public figures must prove actual malice.
Defendant knew the statement was false, or
Defendant acted with reckless disregard for truth
Actual malice = knew false or seriously doubted truth.

blocks.text: Public officials and public figures must prove actual malice.
Defendant knew the statement was false, or
Defendant acted with reckless disregard for truth
Actual malice = knew false or seriously doubted truth.

sections.title: 54. Libel and Slander
body: Written, printed, recorded, or permanent defamation.

blocks.text: Written, printed, recorded, or permanent defamation.

sections.title: 55. Defamation Defenses
body: Applies when statement is made in good faith for a proper purpose.
Absolute privilege is bulletproof. Qualified privilege can be lost.

blocks.text: Applies when statement is made in good faith for a proper purpose.
Absolute privilege is bulletproof. Qualified privilege can be lost.

sections.title: 57. Intrusion
body: Intentional invasion into private affairs that would be highly offensive.
Maya secretly installs a microphone in Leo’s bedroom.

blocks.text: Intentional invasion into private affairs that would be highly offensive.
Maya secretly installs a microphone in Leo’s bedroom.

sections.title: 58. False Light
body: Publicly portraying someone in a misleading false way that would be highly offensive.
A website uses Leo’s photo next to an article implying he supports a group he does not support.

blocks.text: Publicly portraying someone in a misleading false way that would be highly offensive.
A website uses Leo’s photo next to an article implying he supports a group he does not support.

sections.title: 59. Appropriation
body: Unauthorized use of plaintiff’s name, likeness, or identity for defendant’s advantage.
A cereal company uses Maya’s face on its box without permission.

blocks.text: Unauthorized use of plaintiff’s name, likeness, or identity for defendant’s advantage.
A cereal company uses Maya’s face on its box without permission.

sections.title: 60. Public Disclosure of Private Facts
body: Publicizing true private facts that are highly offensive and not newsworthy.
Maya posts Leo’s private medical records online.
Defamation hates falsehood. Public disclosure hates private truth.

blocks.text: Publicizing true private facts that are highly offensive and not newsworthy.
Maya posts Leo’s private medical records online.
Defamation hates falsehood. Public disclosure hates private truth.

sections.title: 61. Intentional Misrepresentation
body: Knowledge or reckless disregard of falsity
Maya sells Leo a building and says:
She knows the roof is damaged.

blocks.text: Knowledge or reckless disregard of falsity
Maya sells Leo a building and says:
She knows the roof is damaged.

sections.title: 62. Negligent Misrepresentation
body: Negligent misrepresentation is careless false business information.
Defendant gives false information in business/professional setting
Defendant fails to use reasonable care
Plaintiff is within the intended group
Plaintiff relies for the intended transaction
Maya prepares financial numbers for Leo knowing they will be used for a small supplier credit decision.
A bank later uses them for a huge acquisition.
Maya may not be liable for that different transaction.
Negligent misrep = right person, right purpose, right transaction.

blocks.text: Negligent misrepresentation is careless false business information.
Defendant gives false information in business/professional setting
Defendant fails to use reasonable care
Plaintiff is within the intended group
Plaintiff relies for the intended transaction
Maya prepares financial numbers for Leo knowing they will be used for a small supplier credit decision.
A bank later uses them for a huge acquisition.
Maya may not be liable for that different transaction.
Negligent misrep = right person, right purpose, right transaction.

sections.title: 63. Intentional Interference With Contract
body: A defendant is liable if they intentionally and improperly interfere with a valid contract.
Valid contract between plaintiff and third party
Maya knows Leo has a fixed contract with a venue.
Maya pays the venue to break Leo’s contract and book her instead.

blocks.text: A defendant is liable if they intentionally and improperly interfere with a valid contract.
Valid contract between plaintiff and third party
Maya knows Leo has a fixed contract with a venue.
Maya pays the venue to break Leo’s contract and book her instead.

sections.title: 64. Interference With Prospective Economic Advantage
body: Plaintiff must show defendant intentionally interfered with expected business.
Maya lies to a potential customer that Leo’s bakery failed a health inspection.
No contract yet = prospective advantage, harder claim.

blocks.text: Plaintiff must show defendant intentionally interfered with expected business.
Maya lies to a potential customer that Leo’s bakery failed a health inspection.
No contract yet = prospective advantage, harder claim.

sections.title: 65. Trade Secrets
body: Defendant took it by improper means
Leo keeps a secret recipe in a restricted digital file.

blocks.text: Defendant took it by improper means
Leo keeps a secret recipe in a restricted digital file.

sections.title: 66. Trade Libel
body: Trade libel is a false statement about plaintiff’s product or business quality that causes special damages.
Trade libel = false attack on product or business.

blocks.text: Trade libel is a false statement about plaintiff’s product or business quality that causes special damages.
Trade libel = false attack on product or business.

sections.title: 67. Slander of Title
body: Slander of title is a false statement attacking ownership or title to property.
Maya falsely records a document saying she owns Leo’s warehouse.
Slander of title = false attack on ownership.

blocks.text: Slander of title is a false statement attacking ownership or title to property.
Maya falsely records a document saying she owns Leo’s warehouse.
Slander of title = false attack on ownership.

sections.title: 68. Malicious Prosecution
body: Malicious prosecution is wrongfully starting a legal case without probable cause, for improper purpose, where the case ends in plaintiff’s favor.
Defendant started or continued legal action
Maya files a baseless criminal complaint against Leo to punish him.
Malicious prosecution = bad case, no probable cause, favorable ending.

blocks.text: Malicious prosecution is wrongfully starting a legal case without probable cause, for improper purpose, where the case ends in plaintiff’s favor.
Defendant started or continued legal action
Maya files a baseless criminal complaint against Leo to punish him.
Malicious prosecution = bad case, no probable cause, favorable ending.

sections.title: 69. Abuse of Process
body: Abuse of process is using legal process for an improper purpose after it has begun.
Maya files a normal lawsuit but uses subpoenas only to pressure Leo into selling his business.
Malicious prosecution = wrongfully start case. Abuse of process = misuse legal tools.

blocks.text: Abuse of process is using legal process for an improper purpose after it has begun.
Maya files a normal lawsuit but uses subpoenas only to pressure Leo into selling his business.
Malicious prosecution = wrongfully start case. Abuse of process = misuse legal tools.

sections.title: Final MBE Attack Plan
body: Step 1: Identify the Type of Harm
Contact or threat: battery or assault
Dangerous activity, animal, product: strict liability
Contract or business relationship: interference tort
Bad lawsuit/legal pressure: malicious prosecution or abuse of process
Intentional torts may allow nominal damages.
Products tort usually requires personal injury or property damage.
Defamation and privacy have special damage rules.

blocks.text: Step 1: Identify the Type of Harm
Contact or threat: battery or assault
Dangerous activity, animal, product: strict liability
Contract or business relationship: interference tort
Bad lawsuit/legal pressure: malicious prosecution or abuse of process
Intentional torts may allow nominal damages.
Products tort usually requires personal injury or property damage.
Defamation and privacy have special damage rules.

sections.title: Final Memory Sheet
body: Trespass to chattels = minor stuff interference
I FLAP = Intrusion, False Light, Appropriation, Public disclosure
Interference with contract = known contract + improper interference
Prospective advantage = expected business + wrongful interference
Trade secret = protected secret stolen improperly
Abuse of process = misuse legal process

blocks.text: Trespass to chattels = minor stuff interference
I FLAP = Intrusion, False Light, Appropriation, Public disclosure
Interference with contract = known contract + improper interference
Prospective advantage = expected business + wrongful interference
Trade secret = protected secret stolen improperly
Abuse of process = misuse legal process
